summary: Gruber's detailed review of the iPhone XS and XS Max focuses overwhelmingly on the camera system as the defining upgrade. He argues that Smart HDR powered by the A12 Neural Engine produces dramatically better photos in difficult lighting, turning previously unusable shots into good ones. He reveals that the wide-angle sensor is 32 percent larger than the iPhone X's — a fact Apple barely marketed — and explains the optical engineering that made this possible without increasing phone thickness. He notes the phones are solid S-cycle refinements overall, but the camera is the singular reason for iPhone X owners to upgrade, and observes that the upcoming cheaper iPhone XR shares the same A12 chip and larger sensor.
key_points: Smart HDR, powered by the Neural Engine, produces dramatically better photos in harsh lighting conditions — turning blown-out or unusable shots into good ones without any post-processing.
The iPhone XS wide-angle camera sensor is 32 percent larger than the iPhone X's, a remarkable improvement Apple barely mentioned in its marketing.

Apple's computational photography advantage is systemic — the Neural Engine processes data directly from sensors rather than post-processing finished images, which Android competitors can't easily replicate.
Every iPhone generation since the iPhone 6 has gotten thicker, reversing Apple's thinness obsession in favor of better batteries and cameras.
The upcoming iPhone XR shares the A12 chip and larger sensor at $250-$350 less, making it potentially the better value for most buyers.
money_quotes: The iPhone XS can capture still images and video that the iPhone X cannot.
In the long run, the smart money is to bet on silicon and software.
One method is to create an image and then apply machine learning to it. The other is to apply machine learning to create the image.
The whole I/O path between the sensor and the Neural Engine is so fast the iPhone XS camera system can manipulate 4K video frames like Neo dodging bullets in The Matrix.
It makes no more sense to review the iPhone XS and XS Max as different devices than it would to write separate reviews of medium and large cups of the same coffee.
12 megapixels are plenty — what phone cameras need are bigger pixels.
Apple isn't celebrating this new sensor, but photographers will be.

iPhones can't compete with big dedicated cameras in lens or sensor quality. It's not even close. The laws of physics prevent it. But those traditional camera companies can't compete with Apple in custom silicon or software.
key_insight: The iPhone XS's leap in photo quality comes from Apple's unique integration of a 32-percent-larger sensor with the Neural Engine's real-time computational photography — a systems-level advantage that competitors cannot easily replicate by buying off-the-shelf components.
